speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. Survivor and war crimes trials participant is Lawrence Rhee. And the date is April 9, 1996. We left off on the last tape, you were starting to state an incident that you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that took place while you were still in military intelligence. And you started with Hitler's last birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I forget the name of the place where this happened in Germany. But anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the SS had gone underground behind our lines, the local SS people. And a German communist, a prisoner of war-- not a-- a civilian had come up and told us that-- what he had picked up information.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we dropped everything else, and were able to get the three head guys, the leaders. First two, and then the third one, I went to his house several times, and he was never there. But his house was well stocked, as I remember, with black market supplies that nobody else in Germany [LAUGHS] had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, in the meantime, the other two guys had given us information that they had a cage of arms hidden in the ground not too far from the-- where we-- where this other guy lived. So we put a few prisoners of war to work starting to dig out. And then I went back again, and this time I did find the guy. And I said, I took my-- it wasn't my .45. It was somebody else's .45. And I pointed it at him. And I said, you go 10 feet ahead of me. I'm not going to give you any instructions where to go. You know exactly where I want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he just took-- made a beeline to the cache of arms. And there we found bazookas, rifles, bullets, dynamite caps, and the Nazi flag that I have there, that I kept because I figured it-- that is mine. So I told him, now we have all these things here. You see what we got here. Now tell me, please, where's the dynamite? The dynamite caps by themselves are practically harmless.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I don't know. And since he was not a prisoner of war, because the he was not in uniform, he was a civilian. I hit him. And I hit him hard. And of course, if you have 20 GIs standing around, then there's no particular heroism [LAUGHS] about that. But I wanted to. And he said, comrade, don't hit me. And every time he called me a comrade, [LAUGHS] I hit him again, because he was no comrade of mine. And he finally said, I'll tell you. I'll tell you. And a couple MPs and I went in our jeep. And he took us to some cave some distance away. And there was the dynamite. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really forgot the name of the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it somewhere--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Near the Siegfried--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: --Near the Siegfried Line?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was already behind the Siegfried Line?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was behind the Siegfried Line. But do you remember their names at all, the people that you caught? This man that you caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But that was a very satisfactory [LAUGHS] moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: After you were done interrogating the prisoners of war, what happened to them? Where were they taken? They were usually taken behind the lines into prisoner of war camps. And they were-- from there on, in bigger units, they were sent back behind the lines. [INTERPOSING VOICES] So your involvement ended with the interrogation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's another odd incident. This was still in Alsace-Lorraine. We had taken this little village. And-- excuse me-- and all of a sudden we got word that our tanks had to disperse because the Germans were planning an air attack on our tanks, a special-- excuse me. And when the people in the village saw that the tanks were pulling back, they thought the whole outfit was pulling back, and they got scared that the Germans were coming back. And some of them came to us and said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the only person that could given-- had-- could have given the German-- they want the German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is a fellow here by the name of Hittler. We thought, they brought Hitler. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told us where he lived. We went there with a couple MPs. We picked him up, and started to interrogate him. And the funny thing is every time we called him Herr Hitler, he spelled his name with double T, and he said, but with two Ts please. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we interrogated him, an innocent little fellow. I just happened to have the name. So we went to visit his place. And he was-- said he was a wine ma-- you know, in the wine-- in wine brewery or distilling. And he had these huge barrels. And you have seen these huge wine barrels where they locate the-- I mean, keep-- keep the wine. He said, here, that's what you see. It turned out the wine barrels were full, full of electronic equipment so [LAUGHS] he could notify the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you discover that? You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [LAUGHS] that's what was found.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to this man, to Hittler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we said-- you see, we were not trained for that kind of interrogation. Once we had what we thought was the-- our-- we took him or had him sent right back to division headquarters, and later on even to core headquarters, because that's more of, of another area of interrogation. But seventh Army-- or our corps got-- got him. But Herr Hittler was quite a find. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else that you can recall about being an interrogator? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Any other unusual incidents that stand out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, these were-- I guess these were the unusual, the somewhat out of [LAUGHS] order.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you didn't actually liberate a camp, but you saw people walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you at all stop to talk to anybody? We talked to them, but since we were on a-- advancing-- we were advanced. We had to keep up-- keep up with the rest of them. All we could say-- we gave them maybe C-ration or D, whatever we had. And-- but we had to keep on going. And you did not recognize anybody, did you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war was over? What did you continue to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the camps were liberated, where were you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sent to a little place near the spa of Wiesbaden, where they had all the military intelligence personnel gather up. There were-- I don't know how many-- but a big group of us. And there I heard that Nuremberg was looking for interpreters and translators and so forth and so on. And I volunteered again. And that's how I got to Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this part of being in the military or was this separate now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was part of the military, but the military intelligence personnel was kept separate from the rest of the troops. To keep them together rather, not, not any separation or segregation. It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you apply alone or did other people also apply with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other people did too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the application process like? What did you have to do to apply?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just forget. I just told them that I'd be willing to go to Nuremberg. And that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were sent as a part of the military, is that-- that's what I'm trying to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still in the military, yes. I was not discharged.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the Army sent you to Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Army transferred me. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Nuremberg from where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Wiesbaden, I don't know. It's-- it wasn't too far. But I forget really. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like coming to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange. [LAUGHS] The city was a shambles. And we were quartered in a place they called Boys Town, where the non-commissioned officers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had their quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your rank at this point?

speaker: interviewer
text: T/5. And it was called Boys Town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys Town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you described the accommodation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a bunch of soldiers and ex-soldiers lived there, and non-commissioned officers. The lieutenants and above lived in different quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know or hear anything prior to this at all about the war crimes proceedings before you joined them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any idea of what you're getting involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were your expectations when you applied for this? What did you think you were going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I was going-- I had hoped I was going to be an interpreter, but I was made a translator. I tried it out for interpreter once, but I didn't cut the mustard. [LAUGHS] It's a very difficult thing to hear one language over the earphone, and speak simultaneously into a microphone in another language. And I, I didn't qualify. But a funny incident again, one of the interpreters there was a person who had been born in White Russia. And somehow gotten out of Russia and had lived in England. And he was one of those people that knew just about every European language that needed to be known. He spoke English, Russian, of course, Spanish, Italian, German. And I think a few-- think-- a few other Slavic languages thrown in. Anyhow, the interpreters only worked every other day. On their off day, they took a practice session during the court's lunch hour. They were given difficult legal terms to practice. In other words, somebody told them something and they had to get it in. And on this particular day, this person, while talking, while listening Russian, talking English, a friend of his gave him a note written in French. And while working those two languages in his mind, he answered in writing in French. It's phenomenal. Really phenomenal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the very first day that you arrived at the Nuremberg trials? What was it like? What do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was assigned to a translating section. And the person in charge there at that time, if I remember right, was Lieutenant [? Niebergall ?], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who has since died, but whose wife I met in Wash-- these-- and whose sister-in-law was another secretary lives here in town, Mrs. Ivy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, they put us to work getting us acquainted. And, and another person that worked there for a while was the author of the book Also, a, a movie was made of it. You remember that? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sam-- the name escapes me now, but

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember my big supervisor was Colonel Kellogg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kellogg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kellogg, yeah. He was immediate over the interpretation, over our section. Photograph, later on photographic evident.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was security arranged for personnel that what there like yourself? What kind of security was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the persons examining your pass, your little card.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were given a pass?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have it over there. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was about it. And I never have seen anybody being frisked for weapons. But I know that one of the reporters-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- after a while, security got lacks. You know how all of the GIs-- put a dog's picture on his ID. [LAUGHS] And he got in that way. And then he wrote about it in the papers. And then all hell broke loose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a GI from the first division.

speaker: interviewer
text: The facilities that you were staying in while you were at the trials, was that far from the courtroom? Were you transported back and forth, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: How do we go? I think we took a streetcar. The streetcars were running by that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, were you able to correspond with people back home? Were you writing letters regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: And receiving mail regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which trials were you involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first trials, the, the Goering, the German government trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly was your position and your responsibility in the first trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well as I've mentioned before, first-- excuse me-- I started out as a translator. But then photographic evidence needed help in translating the German propaganda film, And after about six weeks of doing that, photographic evidence wanted me to stay on. They transferred me to photographic evidence. And there, directly under me, worked Heinrich Hoffmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was over you? Who was your supervisor?

speaker: interviewee
text: The immediate su-- was it-- Colonel Kellogg. Yeah, Colonel Kellogg was over the-- all-- over all that section--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the photographic evidence?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of photographic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was your supervisor. And immediately under you was who?

speaker: interviewee
text: McFadden, I think, was a lieutenant or captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was underneath you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [INTERPOSING VOICE] above me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was above you. McFadden and Kellogg or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you know, you go down the ladder [LAUGHS] or up the ladder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And beneath you was who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other people working in there at that time, we were all of about the same call it category, rank. And-- but Heinrich Hoffmann worked directly under me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Heinrich Hoffmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heinrich Hoffmann was Hitler's personal photographer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how would you describe a person when you're-- in the beginning, our relationship was strictly business. I mean-- He did what we asked him to do, identified persons on pictures in certain situations, and so forth and so on. But he, personally, did not photograph any of the atrocities. They were-- he had no-- in his personal files as far of what I saw of it, were no atrocity pictures. There were all these bigwigs getting together and things like that. But he had a tremendous knowledge of people in and around Hitler. And he was a pretty, pretty slick operator. I mean, he knew how to get around. But after a while-- and I couldn't give-- couldn't give you the reason-- he and I became friendly on the job. I never knew even where he lived. I never visited him or anything. But on the job, we became very friendly. And then little by little, he gave me all these photos that I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he actually under arrest?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, yes. He was a prisoner of war. And he-- in fact, he wore, wore American khaki units, because he had no access to his personal belongings. So US-- the Army or whoever supplied him with the-- American uniforms, if you wish to call it such. And he was under constant guard. There was a guard on him. He couldn't even go to the toilet by himself. He was under constant guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the two of you work together? Were there other people with you or was it just the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were other people with us, but somehow I-- I don't know. We were-- we hit it off. Let's put it that way. Whether he had remorse over what had happened, I'm in no position to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of things did he talk to you about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the movie that-- in the documentary that-- we talked a lot about Hitler. And one day, I asked him, you know, it had been rumored that Hitler's sex life was not of the normal kind. A lot of people had heard about it. And so one day I asked him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me that Eva Braun had told him that they had oral sex. Now Eva Braun used to be Hoffmann's employee. She worked in his photo lab. And one day Hitler walked in, and he said to Hoffmann, who's that lady over there? And Hoffman introduced them to each other. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she became his mistress.

speaker: interviewee
text: And until a few hours before their death was his mistress, until they were actually married. And then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but her married name didn't last but about two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Hoffmann talk anymore about Eva Braun and Hitler? Did he share any other stories about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not much really. Because-- he just told me that he introduced them and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What actually were your responsibilities with the photographic evidence? What did you actually do?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get into the courtroom incriminating evidence pictures, like the atrocities that you have seen and you-- and also, for instance, it was-- it so happened we had the lamp shades that Ilse Koch, of-- who was the commander of Buchenwald camp, had made of human skins. When she saw some better than average looking guys come in, she had them separated right away, had shot them-- had them shot, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had them skinned, then had the skins tanned. And of these, then leather, she had made lamp shades. And I myself carried two of these lamp shades into the courtroom. It was-- and also, we had to produce organizational charts, anything that you could look at and use as evidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lamp shades, for example, how did you get that? Where did it come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they must have come from Buchenwald. I, personally, do not know. But since Ilse Koch was camp commander there, they must have come from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get some of the other evidence that you used? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Yugoslavs came to us with their pictures for reproduction, of which you saw copies. They needed reproduction when their case came up. So the judges got the pictures, and the defense lawyers, and the prosecution so they would know what it was all about, and reproduce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I said, when the Yugoslav delegation or delegate came in and asked me, can you make us so many copies, I said yes. How about could I have a copy? He said, help yourself. And that's how I got the atrocities in my possession that the Yugoslavs had. So your responsibility was to bring it into the courtroom proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Proceedings. We also presented films. Now I remember [LAUGHS] another funny thing. We had a Russian film to show, and somehow I had put the reel backwards. In other-- it started at the end, or upside down or whatever. Anyhow, it was wrong. And with me was one of the Russian guys that was-- and when-- when I showed him-- when the picture started, it started wrong. And I had to reverse it. And of course, he couldn't speak English, and I couldn't speak-- but he-- he did like this. If I would have done that. [LAUGHS] He said, it would have been my neck. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as I said, organizational charts. And I remember another thing. Ohlendorf, I believe it was, a Nazi SS General who was responsible for millions of killings on the Eastern Front, I was seated from him like four or five feet. The desk on the photo that I have, my desk is just barely visible, I'm not. And he said, and I heard this, the Final Solution was applied to more than 5 million Jews. Now, this is an eyewitness statement that I personally heard. And it struck home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did the trial begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: October [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 16, I believe. I have it written down there. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there somebody in-- I know you mentioned the name earlier of the man that was over you, supervising you. Was he in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kellogg?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the Army? He was your immediate supervisor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think he was a Navy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the actual courtroom, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you have seen the picture about the trials in Nuremberg, the movie-- several years ago they made one-- that was the only thing that was true about it, the courtroom. [LAUGHS]
